1:
“These words spake Jesus, and lifted up his eyes to heaven, and said, Father, the hour is come; glorify thy Son, that thy Son also may glorify thee.” The witnesses receive this as the opening of the great High Priestly Prayer, the longest of Christ’s recorded prayers. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene: having ended the farewell discourse (John 16:33), Christ “turned to speak to God for them” (Henry), offering, Barnes says, “perhaps the most sublime composition to be found anywhere,” in the stillness of the night as they went toward Gethsemane. Robertson dwells on the lifted eyes and the vocative “Father” — Christ’s habitual address — and insists “it is inconceivable that this real prayer is the free composition of a disciple,” being rather the memory of an eyewitness aided by the promised Spirit (John 14:26). The prayer turns on the mutual glorifying: “glorify thy Son, that thy Son also may glorify thee.”

The Eastern Chrysostom marks the pastoral lesson in the act: after saying “in the world ye shall have tribulation,” Christ “turns from admonition to prayer, teaching us in our tribulations to abandon all other things and flee to God.” Calvin, for the Reformed, draws the same lesson for teachers: Christ “betakes himself to prayer, for doctrine has no power if efficacy be not imparted to it from above,” mingling prayer with his teaching. Wesley, for the Wesleyan, notes the “simplicity of appellation” in “Father,” to which the believer draws nearest “when he is fullest of love and humble confidence,” and that “the hour is come” means the appointed time of his passion and glory. The witnesses thus find in the verse the solemn threshold of the prayer. The hour of the cross has come, and Christ asks to be glorified — through the very suffering now at hand — that in his glorifying the Father may be glorified. The petition is not for escape but for the accomplishment of his work; and the mutual glory of Father and Son, which runs through the prayer, is set as its keynote: the Son glorified that he may glorify the Father.
2:
“As thou hast given him power over all flesh, that he should give eternal life to as many as thou hast given him.” The witnesses ground the petition for glory in the universal authority given to Christ for the salvation of his own. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “power over all flesh” (exousia, authority) as the Messianic dominion granted to Christ — Robertson calls it “a stupendous claim impossible for a mere man to make,” made also in Matthew 11:27 and after the resurrection in Matthew 28:18. Ellicott notes “all flesh” renders a Hebrew phrase for all mankind; the authority extends over all, but its saving end is defined — “that he should give eternal life.” Clarke reads the universal scope: Christ received “all the human race… that by one sacrifice of himself he might reconcile them all to God,” having “tasted death for every man” (Hebrews 2:9; cf. Psalm 2:8).

On the recipients of eternal life — “as many as thou hast given him” — the witnesses divide along the familiar lines. Calvin and Gill, for the Reformed, take the “given” of the elect: Christ’s authority is exercised “not so much for himself as for the sake of our salvation,” to give life “to all his people” whom the Father has given him. Wesley, for the Wesleyan, reads the verse the other way: since “the Father had given him power over all flesh, so he gave himself a ransom for all,” a “clear proof that Christ designed his sacrifice should avail for all.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both the ground of the prayer and a point of enduring debate. The ground is plain: Christ asks to be glorified because the Father has given him authority over all flesh precisely that he might give eternal life. The debate is over the “given” — whether the gift of life is to a definite people chosen by the Father (the Reformed reading) or, through a ransom for all, to all who believe (the Wesleyan). All agree that the authority is unto life, and that Christ holds his universal dominion for the saving of those the Father gives him.
3:
“And this is life eternal, that they might know thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom thou hast sent.” The witnesses take this as Christ’s own definition of eternal life — the saving knowledge of God and of himself. The grammatical-historical commentators stress that “know” here means far more than speculative acquaintance: it “includes all the impressions on the mind and life which a just view of God and of the Saviour is fitted to produce,” love and reverence among them (Barnes). Robertson reads the present subjunctive “should keep on knowing,” a living and growing knowledge, and notes the rare phrase “Jesus Christ,” perhaps to be taken “Jesus as the Christ.” Gill calls this knowledge “the beginning and pledge” of eternal life, “the way unto it, and means of it.” The phrase “the only true God” the commentators guard against misuse: Wesley insists it excludes “the false gods of the heathens,” “not excluding the Son and the Holy Ghost, no more than the Father is excluded from being Lord” (1 Corinthians 8:6).

The Eastern Origen, attentive to John’s “niceties of the Greek tongue,” notes the article distinguishing “the God” (the uncreated source) from the Word named God — a distinction of persons, not of inequality of nature. Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that the knowledge is saving because “we are all in death till we are enlightened by God”; where God “has shone, we possess him by faith,” and so “enter into the possession of life.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the very essence of eternal life. It is not a bare future reward but a present and growing knowledge — relational, transforming — of the only true God and of Jesus Christ his sent One. To know God in his Son is at once the way to eternal life and its very substance; for life eternal is nothing else than communion with God, begun in faith now and perfected in glory. The verse, far from setting the Father against the Son, joins them as the one object of saving knowledge, the Father as the only true God and Jesus Christ as the One he sent.
4:
“I have glorified thee on the earth: I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s account of his completed ministry, the ground of his petition to be glorified. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the first clause is explained by the second: “God was glorified in the completion of the Messianic work of Christ” (Ellicott). Gill details how Christ glorified the Father — “personally,” by expressing the glory of his person; “verbally,” by ascribing all praise to him; and “really,” by his deeds, chiefly by declaring the Father’s mind and will. Barnes notes that when Christ says “I have finished,” he speaks of his work as virtually complete, “he probably means to include also his death,” all preparations for it being made (cf. John 19:30). Clarke reads him as “already sacrificed for the sin of the world,” speaking of the work as done because its accomplishment is sure.

The witnesses dwell on the boldness and the humility of the claim. Christ, on the eve of the cross, can look back over his life and say that he has glorified the Father and finished the work given him — a claim no other could make, for his whole life, in word and deed, manifested God to men (Ellicott; cf. John 5:36; John 9:4). Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find in the verse the pattern of a finished obedience. The witnesses thus read it as the basis on which Christ asks to be glorified (v.5): having done all the Father gave him to do, having glorified him on the earth and brought his appointed work to its completion, he may rightly ask to enter the glory that follows. The verse holds before every servant of God the ideal of a life so spent in the Father’s work that, at its end, it may be said the work is finished and the Father glorified — an ideal perfectly realised only in Christ, who alone could say it without reserve.

The witnesses note the calm assurance with which Christ speaks. There is no anxiety, no doubt that the work will be done; he reckons it finished even before the cross, so certain is its accomplishment. This is the confidence of perfect obedience, which looks back over a life wholly spent in the Father’s service and forward to a death already embraced. The verse thus reveals the mind of the Son on the eve of his passion — not troubled, but satisfied that he has glorified the Father and done all that was given him.
5:
“And now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self with the glory which I had with thee before the world was.” The witnesses find in this petition one of the clearest assertions of Christ’s eternal pre-existence and deity. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the plain force of the words: Christ asks to be glorified “with the glory which I had with thee before the world was” — Robertson takes it of “the pre-incarnate glory” and “eternal pre-existence” with the Father. This is no created glory newly bestowed, but the restoration to manifestation of a glory the Son truly possessed before creation; the request presupposes that the Speaker existed, and existed in glory with the Father, before the world was made (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott). The petition is that the glory veiled in his humiliation might now, his work finished, shine forth again.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it of the divine glory of the Son, hidden during his earthly abasement and now to be restored to open display; the prayer is that the Mediator, having accomplished his work, might be exalted to the glory proper to his deity. Cyril of Jerusalem and the others find here a witness to the Son’s eternal being with the Father. The witnesses thus read the verse as the crown of the opening petition. Having glorified the Father on earth and finished his work (v.4), the Son asks to be glorified with the Father — not with a new and foreign glory, but with the very glory that was his own from eternity, before the world existed. The request both completes the prayer for his glorifying and reveals who he is: not a creature lifted to honour, but the eternal Son returning to the glory he had laid aside, the glory he shared with the Father before time began.

The witnesses draw the doctrinal weight of the petition. That Christ should ask for the glory he had ‘before the world was’ proves that he was, and was glorious, before all creation — a truth fatal to every denial of his eternal deity. Yet the prayer also shows his true humanity and the reality of his self-emptying: the glory was laid aside, veiled in the form of a servant, and now, his work done, he asks to resume it. So the verse holds together the two natures — the eternal Son and the obedient servant — in a single petition.
6:
“I have manifested thy name unto the men which thou gavest me out of the world: thine they were, and thou gavest them me; and they have kept thy word.” The witnesses take this as the beginning of Christ’s prayer for his disciples, recounting what he had done for them. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “manifested thy name” as making God known — “the word name includes the attributes or character of God” (Barnes), the full revelation of God that came only through Christ (Clarke). Robertson notes the aorist “I manifested” as another claim of accomplished work (with v.4), and that Jesus “regards the apostles as the Father’s gift to him,” recalling the night of prayer before he chose them. Wesley takes “thy name” of “all thy attributes, and in particular thy paternal relation to believers.” The disciples are described as the Father’s: “thine they were, and thou gavest them me,” given out of the world to Christ.

The Eastern Chrysostom links the verse to v.4: having said “I have finished thy work,” Christ now “explains it in detail,” the work being to “announce the Father to men.” Augustine, for the Latin fathers, observes that if only the eleven were meant, this manifestation would be no great glory; it reaches to all whom the Father gives. Calvin, for the Reformed, finds the first plea for the disciples in their having “embraced the doctrine which makes men actually children of God,” and reads the “given” of the elect, given by the Father to the Son. The witnesses thus find in the verse the ground of Christ’s intercession. He prays for these men because they are the Father’s gift to him, taken out of the world, to whom he has made known the Father’s name and who have kept the Father’s word. The verse sets the disciples apart as a people given, taught, and kept — the Father’s own, entrusted to the Son, and now commended back to the Father in prayer. The manifesting of the name is the heart of the work Christ finished, and the keeping of the word the mark of those for whom he prays.
7:
“Now they have known that all things whatsoever thou hast given me are of thee.” The witnesses read this as Christ’s testimony to the disciples’ ripening faith. The grammatical-historical commentators note the perfect tense: Robertson renders “now they have come to know,” a real if not yet full knowledge (cf. John 16:30); Ellicott similarly, “they have come to know, and do know,” the fruit of their spiritual training, “in its fulness still future, but regarded as in the immediate present.” The content of this knowledge is that “all things whatsoever thou hast given me are of thee” — that all Christ’s teaching, works, and authority derive from the Father (Gill, Barnes). Wesley sums it: they know that all Christ has done and spoken is “of thee, and consequently right and true.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, finds here “the chief part in faith,” which “does not rest satisfied with beholding the flesh, but perceives his Divine power.” When they know that all Christ has is “from thee,” they “feel that all that they possess is heavenly and divine”; and “if we do not perceive God in Christ, we must remain continually in doubt.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a quiet commendation of the disciples laid before the Father. Slow and imperfect as their faith had been, they had now come to a true recognition that Christ is no self-appointed teacher but the One sent and equipped by the Father, in whom all is divine. This knowledge — that everything in Christ is “of God” — is the root from which saving faith grows, and Christ pleads it before the Father as evidence that these men are indeed his own, taught of God and persuaded that in the Son they have to do with the Father himself.

The witnesses note how tenderly Christ speaks of his disciples to the Father. He passes over their dullness and slowness, and dwells on what grace had wrought in them — their having come to know that all he is and does is of God. It is the way of the Intercessor to plead the good he sees in his people rather than the faults; and the verse offers comfort to every weak believer, that the Lord recognises and pleads even an imperfect faith, owning as knowledge what is only beginning to dawn.
8:
“For I have given unto them the words which thou gavest me; and they have received them, and have known surely that I came out from thee, and they have believed that thou didst send me.” The witnesses trace here the steps by which the disciples came to know Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators note the chain: Christ gave them “the words” (ta rhēmata, each word, Robertson) which the Father gave him; they “received them”; they “knew surely” (egnōsan, recognised truly) that he came out from God; and they “believed” that the Father sent him. Ellicott marks the double emphasis — on Christ’s work in teaching (“I, on my part, have given”) and the disciples’ in receiving — and that the matter taught was what the Father “had committed unto Him” (John 12:49). Clarke stresses the purity of the teaching: “a pure teaching immediately from heaven,” with “neither Jewish fables nor fictions of men… mingled with it.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses that Christ “declares that God is the Author” of his doctrine, speaking “in the person of the Mediator,” teaching “nothing but what he had received from the Father.” Wesley notes simply that they “received them by faith.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the anatomy of faith. It begins with the word given — the divine doctrine entrusted by the Father to the Son and by the Son to the disciples; it proceeds to reception, the welcoming of that word; it ripens into sure knowledge that Christ came forth from God; and it is crowned by belief that the Father sent him. The order is instructive: faith rests not on human reasoning but on the received word of God, and issues in the twin convictions of Christ’s divine origin and his divine mission. Christ pleads this faith before the Father as the proof that these men are truly his, having received his words and believed in his sending.

The witnesses draw out the dignity the verse gives to the apostolic word. The words the disciples received were not Christ’s private opinions but the very words the Father gave him; to receive them was to receive God’s own message. So the faith built on them rests on the surest foundation. The verse thus grounds the church’s belief in the divine origin of Christ’s teaching, received and handed on by the apostles, and traces every true faith back to the word given from the Father through the Son.
9:
“I pray for them: I pray not for the world, but for them which thou hast given me; for they are thine.” The witnesses dwell on Christ’s intercession for his own and debate the meaning of “I pray not for the world.” The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic pronouns and the strict present tense — “I am praying for them” — referring to this prayer at this moment (Ellicott). Robertson is careful: “not for the world” means “now at this point in the prayer,” for “in verse 20 Jesus does pray for the world (for future believers),” and “God loves the whole world” (John 3:16), Christ having died and prayed for sinners (Luke 23:34; 1 John 2:1). Clarke and Wesley agree: he is “not yet come to that part” — he prays for the world later (v.21); his praying here for the apostles alone no more excludes the world than his later praying for future believers excludes the apostles.

The Eastern Chrysostom reads the prayer as a proof of love: to the still-sad disciples Christ shows his love by entreating the Father for them. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, takes “the world” as “those who live according to the lust of the world, and stand not in the gracious lot of such as were to be chosen.” Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it more strictly: Christ “does not pray for the world, because he has no solicitude but about his own flock, which he received from the Father,” pleading only for “those whom the Father himself willingly loves.” The witnesses thus find here a real and enduring point of difference. All agree Christ is here interceding specifically for his own. They divide on the force of “not for the world”: the Reformed take it of a definite people, the objects of his particular intercession; the Wesleyan and grammatical voices take it as limited to this petition, not a refusal to pray for the world, since he prays for it elsewhere in the same prayer. The verse stands, on every reading, as the great assurance of Christ’s priestly intercession for those the Father has given him.
10:
“And all mine are thine, and thine are mine; and I am glorified in them.” The witnesses find in this a fresh plea for the disciples and a quiet claim of deity. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ speaks here chiefly of persons — the disciples, “all mine are thine, and thine are mine” (Gill) — though the words hold true of all things as well. The mutual possession is offered as a reason why the Father should keep them: they are the Father’s own, and yet Christ’s, so that the Father’s care for his own is a care for Christ’s. Barnes links the closing words to the disciples’ future service: “I am glorified in them” — Christ would be honoured in their lives and ministry (cf. Galatians 1:24; 1 Peter 2:9). Clarke notes that Christ “speaks of the things which were not, but which should be, as though they were,” the future glory reckoned as present.

The witnesses dwell on the weight of “thine are mine.” It is common enough to say that all one has belongs to God; but to say, conversely, that all God’s are one’s own is a claim no creature could make. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here an assertion of the Son’s equality with the Father — the perfect community of possession between them — for only one who is himself God could lay claim to all that is the Father’s. The witnesses thus read the verse as both intercession and revelation. As intercession, it pleads the disciples’ double belonging — the Father’s and the Son’s — as the ground of their being kept. As revelation, it discloses the unity of Father and Son: their possessions are wholly shared, so that the Son’s claim upon all that is the Father’s declares his Godhead. And in these disciples, Christ says, he is glorified — they are the people in whom his honour is displayed, given to him by the Father, belonging to both, and the theatre of his glory in the world.

The witnesses observe how the verse binds the disciples’ security to the union of Father and Son. Because they belong at once to both, they are doubly safe; the Father will not neglect those who are his own and his Son’s together. And the disclosure of the shared possession lifts the eye from the disciples to the Godhead, where Father and Son hold all in common. So the verse comforts the believer with the thought that he is kept by a love common to the Father and the Son, in whom he is owned and in whom Christ is glorified.
11:
“And now I am no more in the world, but these are in the world, and I come to thee. Holy Father, keep through thine own name those whom thou hast given me, that they may be one, as we are.” The witnesses take this as the first definite petition for the disciples — that they be kept and made one. The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast: Christ is “no more in the world” (about to depart, the immediate future spoken as present), but the disciples remain, exposed to trials, and so need the Father’s keeping (Barnes, Ellicott). Ellicott marks the special reference to “the interval between His death and the day of Pentecost,” a time of darkness and danger. Robertson notes “Holy Father” occurs “only here in the N.T.,” the holiness fitting a prayer for the keeping of his own amid an unholy world. Henry observes that all the petitions “relate to spiritual blessings” — not riches or honour, but to be “kept from sin… and brought safely to heaven.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, gives the reason for the earnestness: the disciples “will very soon be deprived of his bodily presence, under which they had reposed”; as a hen gathers her chickens (Matthew 23:37), so he had sheltered them, and now asks the Father to guard them. Wesley reads “keep them through thy name” as “thy power, mercy, wisdom,” and the unity “that they may be one… by resemblance to us, though not equality.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the heart of Christ’s intercession for his own. As he leaves the world, he commits them to the “Holy Father,” asking that they be kept by the Father’s own name — his power and character — and that they be made one, after the pattern of the unity of the Father and the Son. The prayer is not that they be spared the world but that they be preserved in it; and the goal of their keeping is union — with God and with one another — modelled on the perfect oneness of Father and Son.
12:
“While I was with them in the world, I kept them in thy name: those that thou gavest me I have kept, and none of them is lost, but the son of perdition; that the scripture might be fulfilled.” The witnesses dwell on Christ’s keeping of his own and the exception of Judas. The grammatical-historical commentators note the verbs: “I kept” (etēroun, imperfect, continued keeping) and “I guarded” (ephylaxa) — Robertson pictures Christ as their “sentinel.” Of the eleven, “none is lost”; the one exception, “the son of perdition,” is Judas, the very phrase later used of antichrist (2 Thessalonians 2:3), with a play on “perished” (apōleto). The phrase, Wesley notes, is a Hebraism meaning “one that deservedly perishes,” as “son of death” (2 Samuel 12:5). Clarke, citing Augustine, holds that Judas “was lost… because he would not be saved.”

The loss of Judas raises the question of perseverance, and the witnesses divide. Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the verse to confirm the keeping of the elect: Christ kept all whom the Father gave him, and Judas, never truly of that number in the saving sense, was “the son of perdition” who perished according to Scripture — his fall no failure of Christ’s keeping. Wesley, for the Wesleyan, reads it the other way: “so one even of them whom God had given him is lost; so far was even that decree from being unchangeable.” The witnesses thus find here, with the keeping of the disciples, a real and enduring debate. All agree Christ faithfully kept those given him, and that Judas’s loss fulfilled Scripture. They divide on what his loss proves: the Reformed take Judas as never of the kept elect, his perdition no breach of the promise; the Wesleyan take him as one truly given who yet fell away. On either reading, the verse magnifies Christ as the keeper of his own, who lost none but the one whose ruin the Scriptures had foretold.

The witnesses note the comfort the verse gives to the church. The same Christ who kept the eleven through every danger while he was with them now commits them to the Father, and the keeping does not cease at his departure but continues by the Spirit. Robertson’s question presses on every believer — is he our sentinel now? The verse assures us that he is: the Shepherd who lost none but the one foretold to perish still guards his own, so that none given him in the saving sense shall finally be lost.
13:
“And now come I to thee; and these things I speak in the world, that they might have my joy fulfilled in themselves.” The witnesses note why Christ prays this prayer aloud in the disciples’ hearing — for their joy. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that he “utters this prayer in their hearing” (cf. John 11:42) “that they may have the support of knowing that He… had solemnly committed them to His Father’s care” (Ellicott); the prayer itself was “a lesson.” Robertson reads the purpose clause — “that they may keep on having Christ’s joy… realized in themselves” — the perfect participle marking a joy brought to fullness (cf. John 15:11; John 16:24). The “my joy” is Christ’s own joy, imparted to and completed in his disciples.

Calvin, for the Reformed, presses the pastoral motive: Christ was “so earnest in praying for his disciples, not that he was anxious about their future condition, but rather to provide a remedy for their anxiety.” Knowing “how prone our minds are to seek external aids,” he “prays to his Father in the presence of his disciples” that, hearing themselves committed to God, they might be at peace. The witnesses thus find in the verse the tender intention behind the whole prayer. Christ is about to come to the Father, leaving the disciples in the world; but he prays where they can hear, that the overhearing of his intercession might fill them with his own joy. The prayer is for their keeping, but it is spoken aloud for their comfort: that they might know themselves the objects of his care and the Father’s, and so have, even amid coming trials, the fullness of the joy that is Christ’s own — a joy grounded not in their circumstances but in the security of being held by Father and Son.

The witnesses draw the lesson that Christ would have his people share his own gladness. He does not pray merely that they be kept from harm, but that they be filled with joy — and not any joy, but his joy, the deep contentment of one who rests wholly in the Father. To overhear his intercession is to learn that we are held; and that knowledge, the verse teaches, is the spring of a joy that the world can neither give nor take away.
14:
“I have given them thy word; and the world hath hated them, because they are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.” The witnesses connect the world’s hatred of the disciples to their having received God’s word and so being separated from the world. The grammatical-historical commentators note the opposition of “I” and “the world”: the hatred “followed necessarily from the fact that Christ had given them God’s word, and that by it they had been separated from the world” (Ellicott; cf. John 15:18–21). Gill explains that the word is hated because it “magnifies the grace of God, and destroys boasting in men,” running counter to the “carnal interest” and “lusts of men.” Robertson draws out the balance of the believer’s position: they are “in the world” (v.13) and sent “into the world” (v.18), yet “not of the world” — not to be like it nor to draw their “spirit, standards, and message” from it, “else they can do the world no good.”

The Eastern Chrysostom finds the hatred natural: “everywhere virtue is wont to engender hatred from wicked men,” who envy “those who desire to live properly.” Augustine, for the Latin fathers, notes that the disciples “had not yet experienced” these sufferings, but Christ, “after His custom, puts the future into the past tense,” foretelling what would come. Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse another plea: it belongs to the Father “to protect those who, on account of his word, are hated.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both the cause of the disciples’ persecution and a ground of their commendation to God. The word given them has set them apart from the world, as Christ himself is not of the world; and that very separation draws the world’s hatred. But the hatred is also their plea: those who are hated for the Father’s word, and who share Christ’s own otherness from the world, may rightly be commended to the Father’s keeping.

The witnesses press the searching truth that the believer’s likeness to Christ is the very thing the world cannot bear. As the world hated the Master because he was not of it, so it hates the disciples for the same reason. The hatred is therefore no cause for alarm but a mark of conformity to Christ; and the verse teaches the church to expect the world’s enmity precisely where it is most faithful, and to find in that enmity a strange seal of its belonging to the One the world first hated.
15:
“I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil.” The witnesses dwell on Christ’s refusal to ask for his disciples’ removal and his prayer instead for their preservation. The grammatical-historical commentators explain why he does not pray that they be taken away: they have a work to do in the world (Ellicott; cf. v.18). Barnes: “it was better that they should endure” their trials “and thus spread abroad the knowledge of his name”; God could easily remove his people to heaven at once, “but it is better for them to remain, and show the power of religion in supporting the soul.” Clarke notes they “must not yet leave the land of Judea” before bearing their testimony. On “the evil,” Robertson observes the ambiguity (as in Matthew 6:13): it may mean the evil deed or “the evil one,” Satan — and in 1 John 5:18 the phrase is masculine, “the evil one.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out wherein the safety of believers consists: “not that they are free from every evil,” but that they are kept through it; the prayer is not for exemption from trial but for preservation in it. The witnesses thus find in the verse the true shape of Christian security. Christ does not ask that his own be spared the world’s troubles, removed to safety, or taken early to heaven; he asks that, remaining in the world and exposed to its evil, they be kept from the power of the evil one. The believer’s safety is not escape but preservation: he is left in the world for the world’s good, to bear witness and to show the keeping power of grace, while being guarded from the malice of Satan. The verse thus rebukes every wish merely to flee the world’s dangers, and sets the disciple’s calling where Christ set it — in the world, against the evil one, kept by the Father’s hand.

The witnesses observe how the verse defines the believer’s calling between two false extremes. He is not to be of the world, sharing its spirit; nor is he to flee the world, abandoning his post. He is to remain in it, kept from the evil one, that he may do the world good. So the prayer maps the whole of Christian existence — a life lived in the midst of danger, neither conformed to it nor withdrawn from it, but guarded by God for witness and service until the work is done.
16:
“They are not of the world, even as I am not of the world.” The witnesses note that this repeats word for word the ground given in v.14, and mark the purpose of the repetition. The grammatical-historical commentators take it as deliberate emphasis: Robertson calls it simply a “repetition of verse 14 for emphasis,” and Gill observes that what was there given as the reason of the world’s hatred is here used “as an argument with his Father, that he would take notice of them, and preserve them.” Ellicott sees the thought advancing: “the thought of their being still in the world leads on to their mission in the world, and the prayer passes from the thought of preservation to that of their sanctification for their work.” Their fitness for that mission is already declared — “already they are not of the world, even as He is not of the world.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads it as another plea to move the Father to favour: Christ “again says that the world hates them,” and states “that this hatred does not arise from any fault of theirs, but because the world hates God and Christ.” The witnesses thus find in the repeated words both a hinge and a plea. As a hinge, they turn the prayer from preservation (vv.11–15) toward sanctification and mission (vv.17–19): because the disciples are not of the world, they are both hated by it and fit to be sent into it. As a plea, they press the disciples’ likeness to Christ as a claim on the Father’s care — those who share the Son’s otherness from the world, and bear the world’s hatred for it, may surely look to the Father to keep and consecrate them. The verse thus restates the great paradox of the disciple’s standing: in the world, hated by it, yet not of it, even as Christ is not.

The witnesses note that the verse keeps the disciples’ otherness ever before the Father as the reason for both their danger and their fitness. Because they are not of the world, the world will hate them, and the Father must keep them; because they are not of the world, they may be sent into it to do it good. The repetition fixes this paradox at the centre of the prayer, and reminds the church in every age that its strangeness to the world is not a thing to be lamented but the very mark of its likeness to Christ.
17:
“Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth.” The witnesses take this as the petition for the disciples’ holiness and consecration, by means of the word. The grammatical-historical commentators note the double sense of “sanctify” (hagiazō): Clarke explains it means both “to consecrate, to separate… and devote to God,” and “to make holy or pure,” and reads the prayer in both senses. Robertson takes it of consecrating or setting apart to God (cf. Exodus 28:41; 1 Thessalonians 5:23), done “in the sphere of truth — God’s truth, God’s Word, not human speculation.” Ellicott prefers “in thy truth,” “the sphere in which their sanctification was to take place.” Henry stresses the making good: “not only kept from evil, but made good,” holiness being their preservation; Barnes notes that sanctification “in the heart of a Christian is progressive,” a growing likeness to God.

Calvin, for the Reformed, joins the two: God sanctifies “when he renews us by his Spirit, and confirms in us the grace of renewal, and continues it to the end”; Christ asks that the Father “consecrate them entirely to himself” and points out “the means” — the truth of his word. Wesley reads both the consecration to office “by the anointing of thy Spirit” and the perfecting “in holiness by means of thy word.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the great prayer for sanctification and its instrument. Christ asks not merely that the disciples be kept from evil but that they be made holy and set apart to God; and the means is “thy truth,” which he at once defines — “thy word is truth.” It is by the word of God, received and obeyed, that the soul is both consecrated and purified; so the verse binds holiness to the Scriptures, making the word the appointed instrument by which the Spirit sanctifies the people of God.

The witnesses press the practical lesson that holiness comes by the word. There is no sanctification apart from the truth; the soul is purified as it receives, believes, and obeys the Scriptures. So the verse rebukes every hope of growth in holiness that neglects the word, and directs the believer who would be made holy to the same means Christ here names — the truth of God, which is the appointed instrument of the Spirit’s cleansing and consecrating work.
18:
“As thou hast sent me into the world, even so have I also sent them into the world.” The witnesses take this as the ground of the prayer for sanctification — the disciples’ mission, modelled on Christ’s own. The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel of the two sendings: Robertson observes that the verb “I sent” (apesteila) is the very word of their original commission (Mark 3:14; Luke 9:2) and of its renewal after the resurrection (John 20:21). Ellicott marks the aorist “didst send,” pointing to “the definite moment of His mission” (cf. John 10:36), and notes that “in the very word ‘Apostles’ their mission was contained.” Gill is careful that the Father’s sending of Christ “does not suppose inequality of nature, nor change of place,” but the manifestation of the eternal Son in human nature under a divine warrant.

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the force of the parallel: “the calling of Christ and of the apostles is the same calling, and is common to both.” Christ in effect says, “I now appoint them to an office which I have hitherto held by thy command; and therefore it is necessary that they should be furnished with the power of thy Spirit, that they may be able to sustain so weighty a charge.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the reason the disciples must be sanctified: they are sent as Christ was sent. The mission grounds the petition — those who go into the world as the Father’s ambassadors, on the same errand and with the same authority as the Son, must be consecrated and made holy for so great a task. The verse dignifies the apostolic (and the church’s) calling by setting it in the likeness of Christ’s own sending, and so makes the prayer for sanctification a prayer for fitness to bear the commission of the One who was himself sent by the Father.

The witnesses draw out the dignity and the demand of the mission. To be sent as Christ was sent is the highest honour and the heaviest charge; it lays on the disciple the obligation to go into the world for its salvation, and the need to be consecrated for so great a work. The verse thus binds together mission and holiness: those sent into the world must not be of it, and must be sanctified for the errand on which the Father, through the Son, has dispatched them.
19:
“And for their sakes I sanctify myself, that they also might be sanctified through the truth.” The witnesses are careful that Christ’s “sanctifying” of himself cannot mean making himself holy, for he had no sin; it means his consecration as a sacrifice. The grammatical-historical commentators agree: Barnes — “I consecrate myself exclusively to the service of God… not personal sanctification, for he had no sin, but setting himself apart entirely to the work of redemption.” Clarke makes it explicit: “I consecrate and devote myself to death, that I may thereby purchase eternal salvation for them,” with “an allusion to the entering of the high priest into the holy of holies… So Jesus entered into the holiest of all by his own blood.” Ellicott and Robertson take it of his consecration “to the work immediately before Him — the offering Himself as a sacrifice” (cf. John 10:36; Romans 15:16). Wesley: “I devote myself as a victim, to be sacrificed.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the source of the disciples’ holiness: Christ “consecrated himself to the Father, that his holiness might come to us; for as the blessing on the first-fruits is spread over the whole harvest, so the Spirit of God cleanses us by the holiness of Christ.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the fountain of all the sanctification prayed for in v.17. Christ sets himself apart as the great sacrifice — the High Priest who is also the victim, consecrating himself to death “for their sakes” — that out of his consecration their sanctification might flow. The disciples are made holy “through the truth,” but the truth’s power rests on this self-offering: it is because Christ devoted himself wholly to God in his sacrificial death that his holiness reaches his people. The verse thus joins the disciples’ sanctification to Christ’s self-consecration, the cleansing of the church to the offering of its Lord.

The witnesses observe how the verse makes Christ’s sacrifice the spring of the believer’s holiness. He does not merely command sanctification; he provides it, by offering himself. The consecration of the Head secures the consecration of the members, so that the truth which sanctifies has its power from the cross. The verse thus roots all Christian holiness in the self-offering of Christ, teaching that we are made holy not by our own striving alone but by partaking, through the Spirit, of the holiness of the One who sanctified himself for us.
20:
“Neither pray I for these alone, but for them also which shall believe on me through their word.” The witnesses note the great widening of the prayer to embrace the whole future church. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the universal reach: Clarke says the prayer “extends itself through all ages, and takes in every soul that believes in the Lord Jesus”; Barnes finds it “a matter of unspeakable joy that each Christian, however humble… can reflect that he was remembered in prayer” by Christ. Ellicott notes that the thought of the apostles’ mission “leads on to the wider thought of the Church which shall believe through their word.” Robertson marks the means — “through their word” (dia tou logou autōn) — “the agency of conversation and preaching, a blessed privilege open to all believers thus to win men to Christ,” though “sadly limited by the lives of those who speak in Christ’s name.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, observes that Christ here “conjoined all others” to the apostles — not only those then alive but those “to be” in every age. The Eastern Chrysostom adds a note of consolation: the disciples were to be “the cause of the salvation of others.” Calvin, for the Reformed, finds here “a remarkable ground of confidence”: if we believe in Christ through the gospel, “we ought to entertain no doubt that we are already gathered with the apostles” into the company for whom Christ prayed. The witnesses thus find in the verse the most comforting enlargement of the High Priestly Prayer. Christ’s intercession does not stop with the eleven; it reaches forward across the centuries to every soul that would believe on him through the apostolic word. The verse assures the humblest later believer that he was present to the mind of Christ in this prayer, named among those for whom he asked the Father — so that the unity, keeping, and glory he sought for the apostles he sought also for the whole church to the end of the world.

The witnesses dwell on the tenderness of a Saviour who, on the eve of his death, looked beyond the men before him to the countless multitude who would believe through their preaching. The prayer thus spans the ages, gathering every future believer into its embrace. And it honours the humble means by which faith comes — the spoken and preached word — making every Christian who tells the gospel a link in the chain by which Christ’s prayer is answered and the church is gathered to the end of time.
21:
“That they all may be one; as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee, that they also may be one in us: that the world may believe that thou hast sent me.” The witnesses take this as the heart of the prayer for the church — its unity, modelled on the union of Father and Son, and aimed at the world’s faith. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the pattern and the means. Ellicott: the unity embraces “both ‘these’ (the Apostles) and… the whole body of believers in all times,” after the model “as thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee.” Robertson presses that “the only possible way to have unity among believers is for all of them to find unity first with God in Christ” — “one in us.” Gill takes the oneness of believers “among themselves… in faith, and in the knowledge of Christ.” Clarke notes it “was literally answered to the first believers, who were all of one heart and of one soul” (Acts 4:32), and asks why believers are not so now — “because they neither attend to the example nor to the truth of Christ.”

Augustine, for the Latin fathers, dwells on the depth of the model — that the union of the church is patterned on the ineffable union of the Father and the Son. Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others note the great end: “that the world may believe that thou hast sent me.” The witnesses thus find in the verse both the church’s highest calling and its evangelistic purpose. The unity Christ seeks is no mere outward agreement but a spiritual oneness, flowing from the union of believers with the Father and the Son — “one in us” — and patterned on the very oneness of the Godhead. And its purpose reaches beyond the church to the world: that the visible love and unity of Christ’s people might be the evidence by which the world is brought to believe that the Father sent the Son. So the prayer that began for the apostles’ keeping rises here to its grandest object — the unity of the whole church, after the likeness of the Trinity, for the conversion of the world.
22:
“And the glory which thou gavest me I have given them; that they may be one, even as we are one.” The witnesses take this as a further ground of the church’s unity — the shared glory of Christ. The grammatical-historical commentators understand “the glory” variously but agree it is something Christ received from the Father and imparts to his people, that they might be one. Some take it of the glory of sonship and the knowledge of God, others of the indwelling Spirit, others of the glory of grace now and of heaven to come; in each case it is a gift flowing from Christ to the disciples, binding them into one as the Father and Son are one (Gill, Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). The repeated end — “that they may be one, even as we are one” — keeps the union of Father and Son as the unfailing pattern.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others find here the means by which the unity prayed for in v.21 is secured: Christ does not merely ask that his people be one, but imparts to them the very glory the Father gave him, that out of a shared participation in his glory their oneness might grow. The witnesses thus read the verse as the supply that answers the petition. The unity of the church is no achievement of human effort but the fruit of a gift: the glory Christ received from the Father he has bestowed on his own, and in their common possession of it they are made one. As the Father and Son are one in shared glory and life, so believers, sharing the glory given them by Christ, are drawn into the same unity. The verse thus grounds the church’s oneness not in organisation or sentiment but in a real participation, through Christ, in the glory and life of God.

The witnesses press the comfort that the church’s unity is a gift before it is a duty. Christ does not lay on his people an impossible burden of self-made agreement, but imparts to them his own glory, in which their oneness is rooted. The verse thus directs every effort after Christian unity back to its true source: not in compromise or contrivance, but in a deeper sharing of the glory Christ has given, by which believers are drawn together as they are drawn nearer to him.
23:
“I in them, and thou in me, that they may be made perfect in one; and that the world may know that thou hast sent me, and hast loved them, as thou hast loved me.” The witnesses take this as the culmination of the unity-petition — the mutual indwelling that perfects the church’s oneness and convinces the world. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the chain of indwelling: “I in them, and thou in me,” so that through Christ the Father dwells in believers, and they are “made perfect in one” — brought to a complete and mature unity (Gill, Ellicott). The purpose is again evangelistic, but heightened: “that the world may know” not only “that thou hast sent me” (v.21) but “and hast loved them, as thou hast loved me.” The astonishing word, Barnes and the others note, is that the Father loves believers with the very love he bears the Son.

Calvin, for the Reformed, and the others dwell on the wonder of this love — that the Father should love the members as he loves the Head. The witnesses thus find in the verse the summit of the prayer for the church. The unity Christ seeks is perfected by the indwelling of God: the Son in believers, the Father in the Son, so that the whole company is bound into one by the very life of God within it. And this perfected unity has a twofold witness to the world — that the Father sent the Son, and that the Father loves the church with the same love he bears his own Son. The verse thus joins the church’s unity, its indwelling by God, and the world’s persuasion into a single design; and it crowns the believer’s assurance with the most amazing truth of the prayer — that he is loved by the Father as the Father loves Christ, drawn by that love into the unity and glory of the Son.

The witnesses dwell on the staggering close of the verse — that the Father loves believers as he loves his own Son. This is the measure of the love that holds the church, and the deepest ground of its assurance. The verse thus lifts the believer’s eyes from his own poverty to the immensity of a love that does not differ in kind from the Father’s love for Christ; and it sets that love, displayed in a united and indwelt church, before the world as the proof of Christ’s mission.
24:
“Father, I will that they also, whom thou hast given me, be with me where I am; that they may behold my glory, which thou hast given me: for thou lovedst me before the foundation of the world.” The witnesses take this as the prayer for the final glorification of all Christ’s people. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the striking “I will”: Barnes and Calvin note it “expresses not a command but a prayer,” a “strong and earnest desire” (cf. Mark 10:35), yet Robertson hears in it “perfect identity of his will with that of the Father” in this moment of exaltation (contrast Gethsemane, Mark 14:36). Wesley marks that Christ “asks, as having a right to be heard, and prays not as a servant, but a Son.” The petition is that his own “be with me where I am” — in heaven (John 14:3) — “that they may behold my glory,” the endless joy of the redeemed (Robertson, Clarke; cf. 1 John 3:2).

The Eastern Chrysostom sees Christ here speaking “of the crowns in store” for his people. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, raises the believer’s hope to its height: the co-eternal Son, equal with the Father, asks that we be with him to behold his glory — a hope “than which there could not possibly be a greater.” Henry, for the Puritan expositors, notes the order: Christ had prayed for their preservation, sanctification, and unity, and “now he prays that he would crown all his gifts with their glorification” — “first for grace, and then for glory” (Psalm 84:11). The witnesses thus find in the verse the consummation of the whole prayer. Having asked that his people be kept, made holy, and made one, Christ asks last that they be brought to be with him and to see his glory — the glory the Father gave him out of an eternal love, “before the foundation of the world.” The believer’s final blessedness is here: not merely to be saved from sin, but to be with Christ where he is, beholding the glory of the eternally beloved Son.
25:
“O righteous Father, the world hath not known thee: but I have known thee, and these have known that thou hast sent me.” The witnesses note the fresh appeal to the Father’s righteousness and the threefold contrast of knowing. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the address: as Christ appealed to the Father’s holiness in v.11, so now to his righteousness (Robertson, the vocative “righteous Father”). Ellicott connects it to the foregoing prayer for glory: the righteousness of God will see to it that those who knew him are not confounded with the world that knew him not. The threefold distinction: “the world hath not known thee” — “but I have known thee” — “and these have known that thou hast sent me.” Robertson notes the concessive force, “though the world did not know thee, yet I knew thee, and these knew.”

Calvin, for the Reformed, draws out the plea: Christ “pleads with peculiar warmth for those whom the unbelief of the world did not prevent from acknowledging God”; it is just that those “who alone know God, whom the whole world rejects, should be distinguished above others.” Wesley adds that “the admission of believers to God through Christ flows even from the justice of God.” The witnesses thus find in the verse an appeal to the divine righteousness on behalf of the believing remnant. Christ sets his disciples, who have known the Father through him, over against the world that has not known him at all, and pleads the Father’s own righteousness as the ground on which these should be received into glory. It is, the witnesses observe, a comfort to the church to know that its final blessedness rests not only on God’s love but on his justice: having known God in the Son, it would be against the righteousness of the Father to leave them with the world that knew him not. The appeal to the “righteous Father” thus seals the prayer for the disciples’ glory.

The witnesses note how the appeal to righteousness completes the appeals to holiness and love that run through the prayer. Christ pleads not only the Father’s love for his people but his justice, that those who have known him should not share the lot of the world that refused him. The verse thus grounds the believer’s hope in the whole character of God — holy, loving, and righteous — and assures the church that its glorification is not only the desire of the Son but the fitting act of a righteous Father.
26:
“And I have declared unto them thy name, and will declare it: that the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them, and I in them.” The witnesses take this closing verse as the summary and goal of the whole prayer — the indwelling of divine love. The grammatical-historical commentators note the two tenses: “I have declared” and “I will declare” — Christ had made known the Father’s name (v.6) and would continue to do so, Robertson says, by “the perpetual mission of Christ through the Spirit” (John 16:25; Matthew 28:20). Clarke specifies the means: “by the conversations he had with his disciples after his resurrection,” and “by the Holy Spirit poured out on the day of Pentecost.” The end of all this declaring is twofold and intertwined: “that the love wherewith thou hast loved me may be in them, and I in them” — that the Father’s love for the Son might dwell in the disciples, and Christ himself dwell in them.

Calvin, for the Reformed, notes that Christ taught “not the sound of his voice alone,” but “employed the secret revelation of the Spirit,” making the Father effectually known. The witnesses thus find in the verse the fitting close of the High Priestly Prayer. All Christ’s work of making known the Father’s name aims at this: that the very love with which the Father loves the Son should rest in believers, and that Christ should dwell within them. The two are one reality — for Christ to be in them is for the Father’s love to be in them, since that love is poured out in the indwelling Son. The prayer that opened with the glory of the Father and the Son closes with that glory’s fruit in the church: the love of the Father, given to the Son from eternity, brought down and lodged in the hearts of his people, with Christ himself abiding there. So the believer’s portion is nothing less than to be loved as the Son is loved, and to have the Son dwelling within — the last and highest answer to the prayer of Christ.
